info.title: Using systemd-coredump to debug application crashes

abstract.para: systemd-coredump collects and displays core dumps to analyze application crashes. A core dump is an image of the process memory at the time of termination.

section.title: Using systemd-coredump
section.para: By default, when a process crashes, systemd-coredump performs the following actions:

listitem.para: Logs the event to the systemd journal.

section.para: You can examine the dump file using coredumpctl or external tools such as gdb or crash.

note.para: Core dumps stored in /var/lib/systemd/coredump/ are by default automatically deleted after three days. This is configured through the d /var/lib/systemd/coredump line in /usr/lib/tmpfiles.d/systemd.conf.

section.para: It is recommended not to edit /usr/lib/tmpfiles.d/systemd.conf directly, as the changes will be overwritten during the next system update. Hence, perform the following:

step.para: Copy the file to /etc/tmpfiles.d/systemd.conf. As files in /etc/ take precedence over those in /usr/lib/, your custom configuration is applied.
Open the file and find the line: d /var/lib/systemd/coredump
Edit the age parameter such as change 3d to 7d for a week or even more using the W notation.
Alternatively, if you want to manage the size of the coredump storage rather than just the time, you should look at MaxUse and KeepFree settings in: /etc/systemd/coredump.conf
